title: Why Do I Defend Decisions I Know Are Wrong?

sections.content:
Three months ago you chose the vendor, the framework, the hire. Now the cracks are showing — and everyone can see them. In the meeting, someone gently suggests you reconsider.

And you hear yourself defending it. Harder than you expected. You list reasons that sounded better in your head. Some part of you already knows you're wrong. But backing down feels like something worse than being wrong — it feels like admitting that the person who made that choice can't be trusted. And that person is you.

sections.title: The Story
sections.content:
In the Indian epic the Mahabharata, two branches of a royal family go to war over a kingdom. On one side stand the five Pandava brothers, who have been cheated, exiled, and humiliated. The greatest warrior among them is Arjuna — a master archer, famous across the land, raised to believe he is a protector of justice.

The two armies face each other on the field of Kurukshetra. Conches blow. Arjuna asks his charioteer, Krishna, to drive him into the gap between the armies so he can see who he must fight.

And there, in that gap, the war stops inside him.

Because across the field he doesn't see enemies. He sees his cousins. His teachers. The grandfather who bounced him on his knee. The mentor who taught him to hold a bow. These are the people he has spent his whole life loving.

Arjuna's bow slips from his hand. His body trembles. He sits down in the chariot and says he will not fight. He builds a tower of noble reasons — it is wrong to kill family, the kingdom isn't worth this blood, surely surrender is more virtuous than slaughter.

But Krishna sees underneath the reasons. Arjuna isn't refusing because he has discovered a higher truth. He is refusing because the man he must become — a warrior who does a terrible, necessary duty — does not match the comfortable self-image he has carried his whole life: the good man, the beloved cousin, the one everyone admires.

To pick up the bow, Arjuna has to let that image die. He has to accept that the right action and the flattering self-portrait are not the same thing.

Krishna doesn't shame him for it. He does something stranger and harder. He tells Arjuna to look clearly — at his actual duty, at what the moment actually requires — instead of clinging to who he imagined himself to be. Act from the situation in front of you, Krishna says, not from your attachment to your own picture of yourself.

Slowly, Arjuna stops defending the version of himself he wanted to protect. He picks the bow back up — not because he stopped caring, but because he stopped needing to look like the person he thought he was.

That is the whole battle, really. The one inside the chariot, before a single arrow flies.

sections.title: The Contrast
sections.content:
The Roman emperor Marcus Aurelius, who ruled at the height of his power, wrote a private note to himself that almost no one was meant to read: "If anyone can show me, and prove to me, that I am wrong in thought or deed, I will gladly change. I seek the truth, which never harmed anyone."

This is the most powerful man alive, deliberately reminding himself that being corrected is a gift, not a defeat. He understood the trap before it caught him: the higher your status, the more your identity fuses to your decisions, and the more it costs to admit one was wrong. So he practiced separating the two on purpose — the choice he made, and the person he is.

You don't defend the decision. You defend the self that made it.

The moment a choice becomes part of your identity, changing your mind feels like erasing yourself. Separate the two, and the decision becomes just a decision again — something you can update without dying.

sections.title: Why It Happens
sections.content:
Two forces lock together here.

The first is cognitive dissonance. When your actions and your self-image disagree, the brain feels genuine discomfort — and the easiest way to kill that discomfort is to bend reality, not the self-image. So you invent reasons. You remember the choice as more justified than it was. You don't experience this as lying; it feels like being right.

The second is identity fusion. We don't hold our decisions at arm's length. We absorb them. "I chose this" quietly becomes "this is who I am." Once that happens, anyone questioning the decision feels like they're questioning you. So you defend it the way you'd defend yourself from attack — with heat, not reason.

The cruel part is that intelligence makes this worse, not better. The smarter you are, the better you are at generating convincing reasons for whatever protects your ego. Your competence becomes the lawyer for your insecurity.

Underneath it all sits a simple confusion: you believe that admitting a wrong decision means you are a bad decision-maker. So your sense of being capable depends on never having been wrong — which is the one thing no capable person can ever claim.

sections.title: The Trap
sections.content:
Overcorrect and you get the opposite disease: the person who abandons every decision the instant it's questioned, who treats their own judgment as worthless, who calls this 'humility.' That's not openness — it's just identity collapse with better branding. People stop trusting your choices because you don't either.

The goal isn't to hold your decisions loosely because they don't matter. It's to hold them firmly while you believe in them, and release them cleanly when the evidence turns — without the release feeling like a death. Conviction and updating are not opposites. The skill is doing both without your sense of self riding on either one.

sections.title: The Move
sections.content:
1. Name the real fear out loud. Before the meeting, finish this sentence honestly: 'If I admit this was wrong, I'm afraid people will think ___.' Usually the word is incompetent, foolish, or unreliable. Seeing it written shrinks it.

2. Separate the decision from the decider. Say it explicitly, even just to yourself: 'I made a reasonable call with the information I had. New information changed the picture.' Past-you is not on trial. Present-you is just steering.

3. Change your mind in public, on purpose. The first time you say 'I was wrong about this, here's what I'm doing instead' — and the sky doesn't fall — the spell breaks. People trust you more, not less. Defending a sinking choice is what looks weak.

4. Pre-commit to the off-ramp. When you make a big call, write down in advance: 'I will reverse this if I see X.' Now changing course isn't a confession — it's you keeping a promise you made to yourself.

5. Reward the messenger. When someone shows you you're wrong, thank them for it visibly. You're training the people around you to keep telling you the truth — which is the only thing that protects you from defending the next bad decision even longer.
